links.text: The Monument to the Great Fire of London — Wikipedia

summary: English Baroque architect who rebuilt 52 London churches after the Great Fire of 1666, most notably St. Paul's Cathedral on Ludgate Hill. Also designed the Monument to the Great Fire, the Royal Observatory at Greenwich, the Trinity College Cambridge library, portions of Hampton Court Palace, and the Wren Building at William & Mary. Before architecture, illustrated Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome, introducing the word 'neurology.'
comprehensive_summary:
Sir Christopher Wren was an English Baroque architect who became the preeminent builder of his era following the Great Fire of London in 1666. He was commissioned to redesign 52 London churches, with St. Paul's Cathedral — his masterpiece on Ludgate Hill — standing as his greatest achievement. Nikolaus Pevsner dubbed its dome 'the most perfect in the world.' Wren's tomb is contained within St. Paul's, and its Latin inscription reads: 'Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you.' The cathedral is an English Baroque edifice featuring woodwork by Grinling Gibbons and ironwork by Jean Tijou, domes inspired by Mansart's Val-de-Grace and also the Romanesque lantern at Ely (where Wren's uncle was bishop), a facade with paired Corinthian columns, and a whispering gallery below its three-shelled double dome. Its dome is also flanked by two spires and surrounded by a Stone Gallery (above which lies a Golden Gallery). The cathedral's eastern limewood paneling incorporates a rocket and the names of 28,000 American soldiers who died in Britain during World War II. Also interred within is John Donne. Herbert Mason's famous 1940 photograph of St. Paul's surrounded by Blitz smoke became an iconic image of resilience. Bill Viola's Martyrs and William Holman Hunt's The Light of the World are among the artworks housed inside.

Wren also designed the Monument to the Great Fire — a fluted Doric column 202 feet tall with an interior staircase allowing visitors to reach a golden urn of fire at the top. Wren served as MP for Weymouth and used his access to Portland stone from the local quarries for St. Paul's.

The Royal Observatory at Greenwich was another Wren commission, built to house John Flamsteed's astronomical work; Flamsteed became its first resident. Tall windows were installed in the Octagon Room for the work. Greenwich also marks the prime meridian. Wren worked with Robert Hooke to reconstruct London after the Great Fire, and he also took on Nicholas Hawksmoor as an apprentice. He contributed illustrations to Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome, the book that introduced the word 'neurology.' Wren is sometimes credited with being a model for the Provincetown Heritage Museum, which appeared in Charles Demuth's watercolor After Sir Christopher Wren. He also designed the Sheldonian Theatre in Oxford, the library at Trinity College Cambridge, portions of Hampton Court Palace (where William and Mary hoped to create an English rival of Versailles, a project abandoned in 1694 leaving a mix of Baroque and Tudor architecture), the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich, and the Wren Building — the oldest collegiate building in the US — at the College of William and Mary. A false ceiling in Salisbury Cathedral conceals beams installed by Wren to help support the weight of its tower. John Dryden's poem Annus Mirabilis commemorates Wren's works. Peter Ackroyd's novel Hawksmoor follows a detective solving murders at churches associated with a Satanist who worked alongside Wren.

works.name: St. Paul's Cathedral
works.indicator: Rebuilt after the Great Fire of London; whispering gallery; dome on Ludgate Hill
works.description: St. Paul's Cathedral on Ludgate Hill is Wren's most celebrated work, rebuilt after the Great Fire of 1666. Its dome — considered 'the most perfect in the world' by Nikolaus Pevsner — rises above a whispering gallery and features woodwork by Grinling Gibbons, ironwork by Jean Tijou, and a facade with paired Corinthian columns. Wren's tomb is inside, with the inscription 'Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you.' John Donne is also interred within. The dome was inspired by Mansart's Val-de-Grace and by the Romanesque lantern at Ely Cathedral, where Wren's uncle was bishop. Its eastern limewood paneling lists the names of 28,000 American soldiers. Herbert Mason's 1940 Blitz photograph of the cathedral surviving became an iconic image of wartime resilience.

examples: name this cathedral that was redesigned after the Great Fire of London by Sir Christopher Wren
rebuilt by Christopher Wren after the Great Fire of London

clues.clue: Contains a Whispering Gallery below its dome
examples: Below that dome, the interior of this building contains a Whispering Gallery.
Above the keystones of this building's interior arches is the 'Whispering Gallery,' which produces unusual acoustics.

clues.clue: Sits atop Ludgate Hill in London
examples: name this Anglican cathedral on Ludgate Hill that was rebuilt by Christopher Wren after the Great Fire of London

clues.clue: Wren's tomb inside reads 'Reader, if you seek his monument, look around you'
examples: The tomb of this building's architect is contained within and reads 'reader, if you seek his monument - look around you'

clues.clue: Dome inspired by Mansart's Val-de-Grace and the Romanesque lantern at Ely (where Wren's uncle was bishop); paired Corinthian columns on facade
examples: The dome of this building was inspired by the Romanesque lantern at Ely, where the architect's uncle was bishop.
He may be most famous for a building with woodwork by Grinling Gibbons, domes inspired by Mansart's...

clues.clue: Woodwork by Grinling Gibbons and ironwork by Jean Tijou inside
examples: this building, which features metalwork by Jean Tijou and a chapel memorializing Americans who died in Britain during World War II
He may be most famous for a building with woodwork by Grinling Gibbons...

clues.clue: Herbert Mason's 1940 Blitz photo of it 'surviving' became iconic
examples: Herbert Mason's photograph demonstrating that this building 'Survives' became a symbol of resilience during the Blitz.
The two spires and dome of this building are visible above a thick band of smoke in a 1940 photograph taken by Herbert Mason during the Blitz.

clues.clue: Bill Viola's Martyrs and William Holman Hunt's The Light of the World housed inside
examples: Bill Viola's Martyrs was commissioned for this building
This building houses William Holman Hunt's painting that shows Christ knocking at a door, The Light of the World.

clues.clue: Three-shelled double dome; English Baroque style; Stone Gallery and Golden Gallery viewpoints
examples: This cathedral's massive three-shelled dome offers panoramic views of London from its Golden Gallery and Stone Gallery.
rebuilt with a double dome by Christopher Wren after the Great Fire in 1666

clues.clue: Eastern limewood paneling lists the names of 28,000 deceased American soldiers; chapel memorializing Americans who died in Britain in WWII
examples: The eastern portion of this building includes limewood paneling incorporating a rocket, as well as the names of 28,000 deceased American soldiers.
features metalwork by Jean Tijou and a chapel memorializing Americans who died in Britain during World War II

clues.clue: Also contains the tomb of John Donne
examples: Tombs in this building include John Donne's and that of the architect himself

works.name: The Monument to the Great Fire of London
works.indicator: 202-foot fluted Doric column; golden urn of fire on top; interior staircase
works.description: Wren designed the Monument to the Great Fire of London: a 202-foot fluted Doric column with an interior staircase leading to a golden urn of fire on top.

clues.clue: 202-foot tall fluted Doric column
examples: This man designed a golden urn of fire to be placed atop a 202 foot monument that he designed.
a fluted Doric column with an interior staircase that allows visitors to reach the urn of fire on top; that sculpture is usually called 'the Monument'

clues.clue: Interior staircase allowing visitors to reach the top
examples: a fluted Doric column with an interior staircase that allows visitors to reach the urn of fire on top

clues.clue: Topped with a golden urn of fire
examples: This man designed a golden urn of fire to be placed atop a 202 foot monument that he designed.

clues.clue: Later bombed by Martial Bourdin in what was described as the first international terrorist attack
examples: That building designed by this man was bombed by Martial Bourdin in the first international terrorist attack.

works.name: Royal Observatory, Greenwich
works.indicator: Built for John Flamsteed; Flamsteed was its first resident; marks the prime meridian
works.description: Wren designed the Royal Observatory at Greenwich to house John Flamsteed's astronomical work. Flamsteed was its first resident. Tall windows were installed in its Octagon Room. The Observatory marks the prime meridian.

clues.clue: John Flamsteed was the first resident; designed to house his astronomical work
examples: The first resident of one of this architect's homes was John Flamsteed.
Wren was also commissioned to design this building to house John Flamsteed's scientific work

clues.clue: Tall windows installed in its Octagon Room for astronomical observation
examples: for which tall windows were installed in its Octagon Room

clues.clue: Located in Greenwich; marks the prime meridian
examples: This building also marks the prime meridian.

works.name: Other Works
works.indicator: Trinity College Library; Hampton Court Palace; Old Royal Naval College; Wren Building at William and Mary; Salisbury Cathedral repairs; Sheldonian Theatre
works.description: Other Wren works include the library of Trinity College Cambridge; the Sheldonian Theatre in Oxford; portions of Hampton Court Palace (which William and Mary tasked him to remodel to rival Versailles, a project abandoned in 1694 leaving a mix of Baroque and Tudor architecture); the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich; the Wren Building at the College of William and Mary (the oldest collegiate building in the US); and repairs to Salisbury Cathedral, where he installed hidden beams to support the tower.

clues.clue: Designed the library of Trinity College Cambridge
examples: This architect designed the library of Trinity College Cambridge and the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich.

clues.clue: Tasked by William and Mary to remodel Hampton Court Palace to rival Versailles; abandoned in 1694 leaving a mix of Baroque and Tudor architecture
examples: William and Mary tasked Christopher Wren with remodeling this place in hopes of creating an English rival of Versailles; however, those efforts broke off in 1694, giving this place a mix of Baroque and Tudor architecture.

clues.clue: Designed the Wren Building at William and Mary — oldest collegiate building in the US
examples: The oldest collegiate building in the US, which is located at William and Mary, was designed by this architect
This namesake of the oldest building on the campus of William & Mary

clues.clue: Designed the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich
examples: This architect designed the library of Trinity College Cambridge and the Old Royal Naval College in Greenwich.

clues.clue: Installed hidden beams behind a false ceiling in Salisbury Cathedral to support its tower
examples: A false ceiling in this building conceals a set of beams installed by Christopher Wren, while the pillars they reinforce visibly bend under the weight of its tower.

clues.clue: Designed the Sheldonian Theatre in Oxford
examples: Though he is definitely the designer of the Sheldonian theater, credit for many of his other works are now given to members of his office like Nicholas Hawksmoor.

works.name: Polymath and Other Contributions
works.indicator: Illustrated Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome; introduced the word 'neurology'; worked with Hawksmoor and Hooke
works.description: Before his architectural career, Wren created the illustrations for Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome, the book that introduced the word 'neurology.' He worked with Robert Hooke to rebuild London after the Great Fire and took on Nicholas Hawksmoor as an apprentice. He also served as MP for Weymouth, through which he controlled Portland stone quarries used for St. Paul's. John Dryden's poem Annus Mirabilis commemorates his works. Peter Ackroyd's novel Hawksmoor follows a detective solving murders at churches tied to a Satanist associate of Wren.

clues.clue: Created illustrations for Thomas Willis's Cerebri Anatome, which introduced the word 'neurology'
examples: This man illustrated the book that introduced the word 'neurology'.
The architect Christopher Wren provided engravings for a Thomas Willis book that first illustrated these objects.

clues.clue: Took on Nicholas Hawksmoor as an apprentice
examples: This namesake of the oldest building on the campus of William & Mary took on Nicholas (*) Hawksmoor as an apprentice.
Like Christopher Wren, this man worked with Nicholas Hawksmoor

clues.clue: Worked with Robert Hooke to rebuild London after the Great Fire
examples: This other British architect worked with men like Robert Hooke to construct several buildings in the aftermath of the Great Fire of London.

clues.clue: As MP for Weymouth, controlled Portland stone quarries used for St. Paul's
examples: Whilst MP for a town in this county, Christopher Wren controlled a nearby island's limestone quarries and used that Portland stone for St. Paul's Cathedral.

clues.clue: John Dryden's Annus Mirabilis commemorates his works
examples: Identify this John Dryden poem that commemorates Christopher Wren's works.

clues.clue: Inspired Charles Demuth's watercolor After Sir Christopher Wren (depicting the Provincetown Heritage Museum)
examples: This painter used force lines to crystallize the sky behind Provincetown's Central Methodist Episcopal Church in a water-color work titled After Sir Christopher Wren.
This man inspired the design of the Provincetown Heritage Museum, which appears in a Charles Demuth painting titled After this man.

clues.clue: Peter Ackroyd's novel Hawksmoor follows a detective solving murders at churches associated with a Satanist who worked alongside Wren
examples: He wrote Hawksmoor, a novel that follows a modern detective solving murders at churches designed by a Satanist associate of Christopher Wren.

clues.clue: Lamented 'ladies think nothing well without an edging'
examples: this man, who lamented 'ladies think nothing well without an edging'
